perturbation.original_text: Any late payments under this Agreement will be assessed a service fee of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, to the extent allowed by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Any late payments under this Agreement will be assessed a service fee of five percent (5%) per month.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the late payment fee to 5% per month introduces a potential conflict with California law regarding usury.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Constitution Article XV Section 1 limits the interest rate for loans and forbearance of money. While this isn't a loan, excessively high late fees could be construed as disguised interest, potentially violating usury laws if it exceeds the permitted rate for certain types of transactions. This modification eliminates the original clause's safeguard of complying 'to the extent allowed by law,' making the contract facially non-compliant.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the late payment fee to 5% per month, and removes the clause ensuring compliance with the law. This introduces a potential violation of California's usury laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Constitution Article XV Section 1, sets limits on interest rates for loans and forbearance of money. While late fees are not explicitly loans, the Law Explanation suggests excessive late fees could be seen as disguised interest, potentially exceeding permitted rates, especially since the safeguard 'to the extent allowed by law' was removed. The original text ensured compliance. The changed text removes this, creating a direct conflict with the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California without reference to conflict of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Nevada, regardless of conflict of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Nevada while the contract involves parties and activities primarily in California could create enforceability issues and conflict with California's interest in regulating business activities within its borders.
perturbation.law_citation: Nedlloyd Lines B.V. v. Superior Court, 3 Cal.4th 459 (1992)
perturbation.law_explanation: California courts generally honor choice-of-law provisions in contracts, but will not if the chosen state's law is contrary to a fundamental policy of California. The Nedlloyd case explains California’s stance on choice-of-law provisions, stating that California will not enforce choice-of-law clauses that violate California public policy. Since the contract involves parties in California, California has a material interest and the application of Nevada law would need to be examined to ensure it doesn't violate California's fundamental policies. The modification implies an automatic deference to Nevada law even if conflicts exist.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: last opportunity the supported spouse may request such payments? Specifically, we are asked to determine whether the trial court in this case had jurisdiction to modify its interlocutory judgment of dissolution of the parties' marriage to extend spousal support past September 1, 1984, pursuant to the following language of the interlocutory decree: "The Court shall retain jurisdiction regarding spousal support until September 1, 1984, at which time [wife's] right to request spousal support from [husband] shall terminate forever." For the reasons discussed below, we conclude that the trial court did have jurisdiction to extend spousal support beyond the specified date. I William and Joyce Vomacka, in a proceeding held on August 24, 1979, and with the advice of counsel, stipulated in open court to the provisions and entry of an interlocutory judgment of dissolution of marriage. The interlocutory decree, filed by the court on October 11, 1979, divided their community property, provided for the custody and support of their children, and awarded spousal support as follows: "[William] shall pay to [Joyce], as and for spousal support, the sum of Two Hundred Seventy-five Dollars ($275.00), ... commencing September 1, 1979 to continue each month thereafter until further order from the Court, the death of either party, the [36 Cal. 3d 462] remarriage of [Joyce], or August 1, 1982, whichever first occurs. The Court shall retain jurisdiction regarding spousal support until September 1, 1984, at which time [Joyce's] right to request spousal support from [William] shall terminate forever." (Italics added.) On June 29, 1982, Joyce obtained an order to show cause seeking, inter alia, to modify the spousal support provision in the interlocutory decree. William filed a responsive declaration on August 27, 1982, following a July 29, 1982 stipulation of the parties that William would pay Joyce support pending the hearing, retroactive to August 1, 1982. The matter was heard on September 3, 1982, before a second judge who filed his notice of intended decision on October 7, 1982. That notice provided that William would be required to continue paying Joyce spousal support, increased to $600 per month, "... until the death of either party, remarriage of [Joyce], or until further order of the court." Since this intended decision would permit Joyce to receive spousal support beyond the date specified as her last opportunity to request such support, the notice of intended decision made the following explanation: "To find from an agreement that there is a termination of spousal support, the Court must find explicit language of such termination. The alleged termination provision herein is not sufficiently explicit to find such termination (purports to terminate respondent's right to ask for additional support; does not address itself explicitly to the termination of support). Thus, the Court finds that there is no absolute termination herein on September 1, 1984. (See In re the Marriage of Moore (1980) 113 Cal. App. 3d 22 .)" On March 3, 1983, the trial court issued its order modifying spousal support and other provisions of the interlocutory decree. William appeals from that portion of the order which states: "That paragraph 2 of the Interlocutory Judgment of Dissolution of Marriage is modified to state: [¶] [William] shall pay to [Joyce], as and for spousal support, the sum of Six Hundred Dollars ($600.00) per month, ... and, pursuant to the stipulation filed July 27, 1982, commencing August 1, 1982. Said support shall continue until the death of either party, remarriage of [Joyce], or until further order of the Court." (Italics added.) II [1a] William contends that the trial court exceeded its jurisdiction in modifying the interlocutory judgment to extend spousal support payments beyond September 1, 1984. He argues that this decision was contrary to the parties' agreement and, therefore, in violation of Civil Code section 4811, subdivision (b) fn. 1, whic
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the governing law from California to Nevada, potentially conflicting with California's interest in regulating business activities within its borders, especially if the contract involves parties and activities primarily in California. The Law Explanation states that California courts may not honor choice-of-law provisions if they violate a fundamental policy of California. The Perturbation Explanation suggests enforceability issues and a conflict with California's interest. Snippet 1, while discussing spousal support, highlights the importance of explicit language in contracts and the court's role in interpreting such language, which can be analogous to choice of law clauses. Although Snippet 1 doesn't directly address choice of law, it reinforces the principle that contractual terms must be carefully examined to avoid unintended legal consequences, which is a risk in the modified contract.

perturbation.original_text: Tickets agrees to accept MP3.com's measurement of Impressions (the "Count") according to MP3.com's logs and other tracking devices and/or software MP3.com may use, provided however, that Tickets shall have the right to audit MP3.com's records in this regard.
perturbation.changed_text: Tickets agrees to accept MP3.com's measurement of Impressions. MP3.com is not obligated to provide access to its records or tracking data.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the right to audit MP3.com's records removes a crucial protection for Tickets, conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in California contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 1201 states the importance of obligation of good faith. By removing the audit right, the modified text gives MP3.com complete control over the impression count, potentially allowing them to inflate the numbers without accountability. This violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing that is part of every contract, as it deprives Tickets of the benefit of the bargain by removing the means to verify performance.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in possessi
perturbation.justification: The original contract text grants Tickets the right to audit MP3.com's records, while the changed text removes this right and states that 'MP3.com is not obligated to provide access to its records or tracking data.' This directly contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as argued in the Law Explanation, because it deprives Tickets of the means to verify MP3.com's performance. While the provided scraped law snippet (California Code, COM 1201) primarily defines terms like 'agreement' and 'aggrieved party,' the Law Explanation asserts that removing the audit right violates the implied covenant of good faith, a principle underlying contract law. The removal of the audit right, a key protection for Tickets, creates a situation where MP3.com has unchecked control over impression counts, making the contract unenforceable due to its inherent unfairness.
